content: I am an incoming PhD student in the Department of Statistics at the University of Michigan. I recently graduated from the University of Minnesota - Twin Cities, where I completed my undergraduate degree in statistics, economics, and mathematics.

title: Tim White

summary: Invited to present in session \#193 of JSM 2022 as part of the ASA Medical Devices and Diagnostics Section's student paper competition.
title: Estimating the lifetime risk of a false positive screening test result

summary: False positive results in screening tests have potentially severe psychological, medical, and financial consequences for the recipient. However, there have been few efforts to quantify the long-term risk of a false positive. We seek to fill this gap by estimating the probability that an individual who adheres to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) screening guidelines will receive at least one false positive in a lifetime. To do so, we assembled a data set of 116 studies cited by the USPSTF that report on the accuracy of the primary screening procedure for one of five cancers or six sexually transmitted diseases. We use these data to estimate the probability that an individual in one of 14 demographic subpopulations will receive at least one false positive for one of these eleven diseases in a lifetime. We specify a suitable statistical model to account for the hierarchical structure of the data, and we use the parametric bootstrap to quantify the uncertainty surrounding our estimates.The estimated probability of receiving at least one false positive in a lifetime is 85.5% (±0.9%) and 38.9% (±3.6%) for baseline groups of women and men, respectively. It is higher for subpopulations recommended to screen more frequently than the baseline, including more vulnerable groups such as pregnant women and men who have sex with men. Since screening technology is imperfect, false positives remain inevitable. The high lifetime risk of a false positive reveals the importance of educating patients about this phenomenon.
title: Estimating the lifetime risk of a false positive screening test result

summary: This booklet explores the life circumstances of older persons (defined as persons aged 60 years and above), focusing on those who live alone, in the context of changing household structures in 93 countries from all regions of the world. The analysis is based on harmonized international census data obtained from the Integrated Public Use Microdata Series (IPUMS) at the University of Minnesota. The analysis presented here is crucial for supporting policy responses to the needs of older persons, including the special attention they require during the current COVID-19 crisis, as they are disproportionately more likely than younger persons to become seriously ill and die from COVID-19- related complications.
title: Household structure and older persons
